summary: Machine policy for Windows biometrics as a whole. Good for strict privacy or security baselines, but it disables Windows biometric features such as fingerprint and face sign-in on the device.

setting.name: Windows Biometrics
setting.casual_explanation: Windows can use fingerprint, face, or other biometric features for sign-in and related capabilities. This device policy controls whether biometrics are allowed at all.
setting.why_people_change_it: Some environments disable biometrics for compliance, privacy, or security-policy reasons. Others keep it enabled because biometric sign-in is convenient.
setting.tradeoffs_overview: Disabling biometrics can satisfy stricter policy requirements, but it removes convenient sign-in methods and related biometric functionality.
setting.professional_notes: Biometrics.admx maps the top-level device policy to SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Biometrics\Enabled with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0.

allowed_values.label: Not configured
allowed_values.meaning: The device does not force biometrics on or off through policy.

allowed_values.label: Biometrics disabled
allowed_values.meaning: Biometric features are disabled by policy.

allowed_values.label: Biometrics enabled
allowed_values.meaning: Biometric features are explicitly allowed by policy.

app_current_implementation.notes: The current app writes Enabled = 0 under the Biometrics policy key, which matches the documented disable behavior.

windows_defaults.label: Windows device-policy default
windows_defaults.applies_to: Windows 7 and newer systems with biometric policy support

states.rationale: The device policy is not configured by default.

windows_defaults.notes: This is the policy baseline, not a statement that every device ships with biometric hardware or user enrollment configured.

recommended_profiles.label: Windows default
intended_for: Devices where biometric convenience is wanted
avoid_for: Environments with a policy against biometrics
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want the device to keep its ordinary biometric policy behavior.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You need a hard disable for biometric features.
tradeoffs: Biometric features can remain available if the device supports them.
No explicit device-wide policy restriction is enforced.

recommended_profiles.label: No biometrics
avoid_for: Users who depend on biometric sign-in convenience
recommended_profiles.good_when: You want a clear and enforceable policy against Windows biometrics.
recommended_profiles.bad_when: You want fingerprint or face sign-in to remain available.
tradeoffs: The device follows a stricter policy baseline.

evidence.title: Local Microsoft Biometrics.admx mapping
evidence.summary: The local ADMX file defines the biometrics policy and shows Enabled with enabledValue 1 and disabledValue 0 under SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Biometrics.

evidence.title: Current app implementation
evidence.summary: The provider writes Enabled = 0 under the Biometrics policy key.

evidence.title: Existing tweak source record
evidence.summary: The repo already tracks provenance for this tweak in the current catalog output.

decision.why: Local Microsoft policy files document the exact policy surface, value semantics, and device-wide behavior. The app writes the documented disable value.

validation_proof.exact_quote_or_path: <policy name="Biometrics_EnableBio" class="Machine" ... key="SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Biometrics" valueName="Enabled">; Biometrics.adml: If you disable this policy setting, the Windows Biometric Service is unavailable, and users cannot use any biometric feature in Windows.
validation_proof.notes: Local official ADMX defines the exact key and value name; local ADML confirms that disabling the policy prevents Windows biometric features from being used.
